text: The surgeon initiates posterior rectal dissection at the sacral promontory, entering the holy plane within the presacral space. The proper rectal fascia and presacral fascia are clearly identified during this process.

The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane between the proper rectal fascia and parietal pelvic fascia, moving superiorly and ventrally. The peritoneum is incised at the superior margin of the rectovesical pouch to access the area in front of the Denonvilliers fascia.

The surgeon performs a low posterior dissection starting at the fourth sacral vertebra, cutting through the thick retrosacral fascia, which originates at the periosteum and inserts on the proper rectal fascia. This dissection provides access to the posterior region of the horizontal part of the rectum, known as the retrorectal space.
Waldeyer's fascia is visible covering the floor of the retrorectal space, including the anterior surface of the distal sacrum and the muscles of the posterolateral walls of the pelvis.

The surgeon performs a low lateral dissection, detaching the proporectal fascia from Waldeyer's fascia, which covers the levator ani muscle. The upper left lateral dissection is similarly conducted between the proporectal and parietal pelvic fascia.

Following the high anterior dissection, the surgeon performs a low anterior dissection by sectioning the non-villiers fascia at the base of the prostate, where it adheres closely to the prosthetic capsule. The dissection then moves posteriorly to the non-villiers fascia, which laterally covers and protects the neurovascular bundles of Walsh.

The surgeon identifies and cuts the rectal efferences of the inferior hypogastric plexus laterally, facilitating access to the levator ani muscles. The rectum is fully mobilized down to the level of the levator ani muscles below the prostate.

The surgeon sections the Valdia's fascia covering the anacoxygene rhea fei, then detaches the horizontal portion of the rectum, achieving complete mobilization up to the puborectalis muscles, part of the levator ani muscle group.

The surgeon transects the rectum at the anorectal junction using an endogia stapler, approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge. The specimen is then retracted from the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon exposes the pelvic floor, revealing the rectal stump positioned below the prostate. The levator ani muscles are visible, along with the lateral neurovascular bundle and the inferior hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon checks the integrity of the nerves, including the superior hypogastric plexus, hypogastric nerves covered by the parietal pelvic fascia, inferior hypogastric plexus, and S-II to S-IV splanchnic nerves. Following this, a standard stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed, visually connecting the rectal stump to the remaining colon.
The endoscopic view shows the anastomosis at the top of the anal canal, six months postoperatively, with the specimen typically extracted through a suprapubic incision.

The surgeon performs high anterior dissection in front of the de Noveles fascia, noting its thickened and adherent nature at the base of the prostate. The fascia is sectioned, and dissection shifts posteriorly to the de Noveles fascia, in contact with the proper rectal fascia, to fully mobilize the rectum anteriorly. The visual difference between high and low anterior dissection is evident.

The cut edge of the de Noveles fascia is visible, adhering to the prostate. The surgeon sections the rectal deferent branches of the inferior hypogastric plexus to reach the levator ani muscles. The rectal sacral fascia is identified and cut to enter the retrorectal space, followed by cutting the last posterior attachment of the proper rectal fascia to the valediers.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic sectioning of the left puborectalis muscle, posterolaterally, to facilitate dissection in the perineal region and achieve a safe circumferential margin. The laparoscopic magnification provides enhanced visualization of the sectioning area compared to an open procedure.

The surgeon performs a standard intercentral dissection between the internal and external anal sphincters, starting at the dentate line and extending posteriorly and laterally. On the left side, the dissection is extended laterally to include the deep part of the external anal sphincter.

The surgeon completes the anterior mobilization of the rectum, ensuring a distal margin of at least one centimeter, confirmed by frozen sections. The rectal specimen is extracted transanally using a pull-through technique. Following preparation of the mesorectum and inspection of the resection area, a standard end-to-end Park's manual colloidal anastomosis is performed.

The video shows the postoperative aspect of the abdomen with a temporary loop ileostomy. An endoscopic view reveals the anastomosis located one centimeter south-left from the anipotaneous line, observed six months after surgery.

The surgeon sections the thickened Waldeyer's fascia posterolaterally to expose the fibers of the Levatorani muscle. Using a laparoscopic approach, the Levatorani is sectioned under direct vision to obtain a large safe circumferential margin in a cylindrical specimen, revealing the ischorectal adipose tissue. The posterior levator is sectioned in front of the coccyx.

On the left side, the surgeon cuts the levator muscle from its attachments at the internal obturator fascia due to tumor invasion. The rectum remains attached to the inferior portion of the vagina, corresponding to the area of tumoral invasion. A large circumferential incision is made to remove the sphincters, mirroring the abdominal dissection.

The surgeon resects the infiltrated posterior vaginal wall en bloc with the specimen, which includes the levator ani muscle. The specimen is transiently extracted, showing the levator ani and posterior vaginal wall included in the resection. The proper rectal fascia remains intact. The final postoperative view shows the perineum closed primarily.
